It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 23 of about 30 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the maze structure clearly. Let me analyze it:
The maze is a winding black path. The interior (dots) is the path where squares can move. The squares are 5x5 and move in steps of 5.
Looking at the structure:
- Rows 9-13: Top bar, solid black (cols 19-53)
- Rows 14-18: Two sections - left (cols 14-26) and right (cols 32-53)
- Rows 19-23: Two sections - left (cols 9-21) and right (cols 38-53)
- Rows 24-28: Two sections - left (cols 9-18) and right (cols 40-53)
- Rows 29-38: Two sections - left (cols 14-18) and right (cols 46-50)
- Rows 39-43: Left square at cols 19-23, right section at cols 40-50
- Rows 44-48: Left section at cols 9-23, right square at cols 35-39
- Rows 49-53: Two sections - left (cols 9-23) and right (cols 32-53)
- Rows 54-58: Two sections - left (cols 9-23) and right (cols 32-44)
The maze has a vertical gap in the middle (cols 24-31 approximately) that runs from rows 14 to 58. The left and right arms of the maze are connected at the top (rows 9-13).
Now, the squares:
- Left square: currently at rows 39-43, cols 19-23
- Right square: currently at rows 44-48, cols 35-39
Looking at the path, the left square is in the left arm, and the right square is in the right arm. Both arms wind upward and connect at the top.
The goal might be to navigate both squares to the top of the maze (rows 9-13). But the top bar is solid black, so the squares can't be inside it. Maybe the goal is to get them as high as possible, or to specific positions.
Wait, looking more carefully at the maze structure, I see that the black regions form a continuous shape. The interior path (dots) is where the squares move. The path winds through the maze, and the squares can move up/down/left/right within this path.
Let me think about the path structure:
- The left arm winds: cols 14-26 (rows 14-18) → cols 9-21 (rows 19-23) → cols 9-18 (rows 24-28) → cols 14-18 (rows 29-38) → cols 19-23 (rows 39-43) → cols 9-23 (rows 44-58)
- The right arm winds: cols 32-53 (rows 14-18) → cols 38-53 (rows 19-23) → cols 40-53 (rows 24-28) → cols 46-50 (rows 29-38) → cols 40-50 (rows 39-43) → cols 35-39 (rows 44-48) → cols 32-53 (rows 49-53) → cols 32-44 (rows 54-58)
The squares start at row 49, which is in the lower part of the maze. They need to navigate upward through the winding path.
Current positions:
- Left: (39, 19) - in the left arm, at rows 39-43
- Right: (44, 39) - in the right arm, at rows 44-48
To move the left square up from row 39, I need to check if there's a path upward. Looking at the maze:
- Rows 39-43: left arm at cols 19-23
- Rows 34-38: left arm at cols 14-18
- The gap between rows 38 and 39: the left arm shifts from cols 14-18 to cols 19-23
So the left square at (39, 19) can move up if there's a connection between rows 38 and 39. Looking at the ascii:
- Row 38: .....#####..............................#####
- Row 39: ##########SSSSS....................##########
At cols 14-18, row 38 has ##### (black). At cols 19-23, row 39 has SSSSS (square). So the black path continues from row 38 cols 14-18 to row 39 cols 19-23. But the square is at cols 19-23, and above it (row 38, cols 19-23) is "....." (background). So the square can't move up directly.
Wait, I need to reconsider. The squares move within the black path. The black path at rows 39-43 is at cols 9-23 (left) and cols 40-50 (right). At rows 34-38, the black path is at cols 14-18 (left) and cols 46-50 (right).
So the left square at (39, 19) is in the left arm. To move up, it needs to shift from cols 19-23 to cols 14-18. This would require a LEFT movement. But LEFT moves the left square left by 5 and the right square right by 5.
Let me check: if I move LEFT from the current position:
- Left square: (39, 19) → (39, 14)
- Right square: (44, 39) → (44, 44)
Is (39, 14) a valid position? Looking at row 39: "##########SSSSS....................##########"
At cols 14-18, row 39 has "#####" (part of the black shape). So yes, the left square can move to (39, 14).
Is (44, 44) a valid position? Looking at row 44: "####################.....#####SSSSS##########"
At cols 44-48, row 44 has "SSSSS" (the right square). Wait, the right square is at cols 35-39. So moving right by 5 would put it at cols 40-44. Let me re-check.
Actually, I think I'm confusing the column indices. Let me be more careful.
The ascii shows cols 9-54. The left square is at cols 19-23 (5 cells wide). The right square is at cols 35-39 (5 cells wide).
If I move LEFT:
- Left square: col 19 → col 14 (move left by 5)
- Right square: col 39 → col 44 (move right by 5)
At row 39, cols 14-18: "#####" (black, part of the maze). So the left square can move there.
At row 44, cols 44-48: "SSSSS" is at cols 35-39. Cols 44-48 would be "#####". So the right square can move there.
OK so LEFT would move the left square to (39, 14) and the right square to (44, 44). Let me try this.
Actually wait, I realize I need to think about this more carefully. The squares move in 5-cell steps. The maze path also has 5-cell-wide sections. So the squares can only be at positions that are multiples of 5 (relative to some origin).
Looking at the maze structure, the valid column positions for the left square are: 9, 14, 19, 24 (approximately). And for the right square: 32, 35, 39, 44, 46, 48 (approximately).
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just try moving the squares and see what happens.
Current positions: left=(39,19), right=(44,39)
Let me try LEFT to see if it works:

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try LEFT to move the left square left and right square right
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
